title: High CPU Utilization
symptoms: System load average > number of cores
root_causes: Runaway process or infinite loop
Insufficient compute resources for workload
Missing rate-limiting on API endpoints
Crypto-mining malware
resolution_steps: 1. Run `top -c` or `htop` to identify the offending process
2. Check if the process is legitimate: `ps aux | grep <PID>`
3. If runaway: `kill -15 <PID>`, then `kill -9` if unresponsive
4. Scale horizontally: add more replicas via orchestrator
5. Apply CPU resource limits in container/pod spec
6. Review recent deployments for regressions
prevention: Set CPU resource limits and requests in Kubernetes manifests
Implement auto-scaling policies
Add rate-limiting and request throttling
Run periodic load tests before deployments

title: Memory Leak
symptoms: Memory usage steadily increasing over time
OOM Killer events in system logs
Application restarts due to memory limits
root_causes: Object references not released (retained caches, listeners)
Connection pool or thread pool leak
Growing in-memory data structures without bounds
Native memory leak in JNI / C extensions
resolution_steps: 1. Capture heap dump: `jmap -dump:live,format=b,file=heap.hprof <PID>`
2. Analyze with Eclipse MAT or VisualVM
3. Identify dominator tree and retained objects
4. Restart affected service as immediate mitigation
5. Deploy fix for the leaking code path
6. Set memory limits and configure OOM behaviour
prevention: Implement bounded caches with TTL eviction
Monitor memory trends in dashboards
Run memory profiling in CI pipelines
Set container memory limits with appropriate OOM behaviour

title: Disk Full
symptoms: Failed log writes and database operations
root_causes: Unbounded log growth without rotation
Large temporary files or core dumps
Database WAL or binlog not being cleaned
Docker images / layers accumulating
resolution_steps: 1. Identify large files: `du -sh /* | sort -rh | head -20`
2. Clear old logs: `find /var/log -name '*.gz' -mtime +7 -delete`
3. Clean Docker: `docker system prune -a --volumes`
4. Rotate and compress active logs
5. Remove old database backups and WAL segments
6. Expand volume if persistent
prevention: Configure logrotate for all services
Set up disk-usage alerts at 80% threshold
Implement automated cleanup cron jobs
Use separate volumes for data and logs

title: Service Crash / Unexpected Restart
root_causes: Unhandled exception or segmentation fault
Out-of-memory kill by the kernel
Configuration error after deployment
Dependency service unavailable at startup
resolution_steps: 1. Check service status: `systemctl status <service>`
2. Review logs: `journalctl -u <service> --since '1 hour ago'`
3. Check for core dumps in /var/lib/systemd/coredump/
4. Review recent config or deployment changes
5. Restart with increased logging: set DEBUG level
6. Rollback deployment if regression identified
prevention: Implement health checks and readiness probes
Add graceful shutdown handlers
Test configuration changes in staging first
Set resource limits to prevent OOM kills

title: Database Connection Pool Exhaustion
root_causes: Connection leak — connections not returned to pool
Slow queries holding connections too long
Too many application replicas for pool size
Connection idle timeout misconfiguration
resolution_steps: 1. Check active connections: `SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity;`
2. Identify idle connections: `SELECT * FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state='idle';`
3. Kill idle connections: `SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid);`
4. Increase `max_connections` in postgresql.conf if needed
5. Deploy connection pooler (PgBouncer) in front of database
6. Fix application connection leak in code
prevention: Use a connection pooler (PgBouncer, ProxySQL)
Set connection pool max-lifetime and idle-timeout
Monitor connection count with alerts
Implement connection leak detection in tests

title: SSL Certificate Expiry
root_causes: Certificate renewal automation failure
ACME / Let's Encrypt renewal not configured
DNS validation records missing for renewal
Certificate stored in wrong path after renewal
resolution_steps: 1. Check certificate expiry: `openssl x509 -enddate -noout -in cert.pem`
2. Renew certificate: `certbot renew` or request from CA
3. Deploy new certificate to all endpoints
4. Reload web server: `nginx -s reload`
5. Verify with `curl -vI https://your-domain.com`
6. Test from external monitoring service
prevention: Automate certificate renewal with cert-manager or certbot
Set up alerts for certificates expiring within 30 days
Use short-lived certificates with automated rotation
Maintain certificate inventory

title: DDoS Attack Mitigation
symptoms: Legitimate users unable to access service
root_causes: Volumetric DDoS (UDP/TCP flood)
Application-layer attack (HTTP flood, slowloris)
DNS amplification attack
Botnet targeting public endpoints
resolution_steps: 1. Enable DDoS protection at CDN/edge (Cloudflare, AWS Shield)
2. Identify attack source IPs from access logs
3. Block offending IPs/CIDRs at firewall: `iptables -A INPUT -s <IP> -j DROP`
4. Enable rate-limiting on nginx/load balancer
5. Scale up to absorb if traffic is semi-legitimate
6. Engage ISP for upstream filtering if volumetric
prevention: Use CDN with built-in DDoS protection
Implement rate-limiting and CAPTCHA for public endpoints
Configure SYN cookies and connection limits
Have a DDoS incident response runbook drilled regularly

title: DNS Resolution Failure
symptoms: Services unable to resolve internal hostnames
root_causes: DNS server overloaded or crashed
Incorrect /etc/resolv.conf configuration
Network policy blocking DNS (port 53)
Upstream DNS provider outage
resolution_steps: 1. Test resolution: `dig @<dns-server> <hostname>`
2. Check DNS pod status: `kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns`
3. Review DNS pod logs: `kubectl logs -n kube-system <coredns-pod>`
4. Verify resolv.conf: `cat /etc/resolv.conf`
5. Restart DNS pods: `kubectl rollout restart deploy coredns -n kube-system`
6. Switch to backup DNS resolver if primary is down
prevention: Deploy DNS with multiple replicas and anti-affinity
Monitor DNS query latency and failure rate
Configure node-level DNS caching (NodeLocal DNSCache)
Maintain fallback DNS resolvers

title: Deployment Rollback
symptoms: Health checks failing for new pods
root_causes: Bug introduced in new code
Database schema incompatibility
Missing environment variable or secret
Dependency version conflict
resolution_steps: 1. Confirm regression: compare error rates before/after deploy
2. Rollback deployment: `kubectl rollout undo deployment/<name>`
3. Verify rollback: `kubectl rollout status deployment/<name>`
4. Check previous version health checks pass
5. Investigate root cause in staging with new version
6. Hotfix and re-deploy when ready
prevention: Implement canary/blue-green deployments
Automate rollback on error-rate threshold breach
Run comprehensive integration tests before release
Keep deployment artifacts versioned and immutable

title: Container OOM Kill
symptoms: dmesg shows 'Out of memory: Killed process'
root_causes: Container memory limit set too low
Memory leak in application
JVM heap sized larger than container limit
In-memory caching without bounds
resolution_steps: 1. Check OOM status: `kubectl describe pod <pod> | grep -A5 'Last State'`
2. Review memory limit vs actual usage
3. Increase memory limit if under-provisioned
4. If leak: capture heap dump and profile
5. Tune JVM: set `-Xmx` to 75% of container limit
6. Restart pod with corrected limits
prevention: Set memory requests = limits (QoS Guaranteed)
Profile memory usage before setting limits
Implement bounded caches and connection pools
Monitor container memory with Prometheus

title: Network Partition
symptoms: Some nodes see each other, others do not
root_causes: Switch/router failure in one rack
Misconfigured firewall rules or security groups
MTU mismatch causing jumbo-frame drops
Cloud provider AZ network issue
resolution_steps: 1. Verify connectivity: `ping`, `traceroute`, `mtr` between affected nodes
2. Check switch/router status and interface errors
3. Review firewall rules: `iptables -L -n` / security groups
4. Test MTU: `ping -M do -s 1472 <target>`
5. Failover affected services to healthy partition
6. Engage network team / cloud provider support
prevention: Deploy across multiple availability zones
Use redundant network paths (bonding/LACP)
Monitor inter-node latency and packet loss
Test network partition scenarios in chaos engineering

title: Slow Database Queries
symptoms: Query response time above SLA thresholds
Growing number of active queries in pg_stat_activity
root_causes: Missing or stale indexes
Table bloat requiring VACUUM
Query plan regression after statistics update
Lock contention from long transactions
resolution_steps: 1. Enable slow query log: `log_min_duration_statement = 1000`
2. Identify slow queries: `SELECT * FROM pg_stat_statements ORDER BY mean_time DESC LIMIT 10;`
3. Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on problematic queries
4. Create missing indexes based on query patterns
5. Run VACUUM ANALYZE on bloated tables
6. Optimize or rewrite inefficient queries
prevention: Schedule regular VACUUM and ANALYZE jobs
Monitor query performance with pg_stat_statements
Set up slow query alerts
Review query plans in CI for critical queries

title: Cache Miss Storm (Thundering Herd)
symptoms: Service latency spike after cache flush or restart
root_causes: Cache TTL expiry for hot keys at the same time
Cache server restart or failover
Cache invalidation of frequently accessed data
Missing cache-aside pattern for cold starts
resolution_steps: 1. Verify cache status: `redis-cli INFO stats | grep hit`
2. Implement request coalescing / singleflight for hot keys
3. Pre-warm cache with critical data
4. Apply jittered TTL to prevent synchronized expiry
5. Scale database temporarily to handle surge
6. Implement stale-while-revalidate cache strategy
prevention: Use jittered TTL: base_ttl + random(0, ttl * 0.1)
Implement cache warming on deployment
Use multi-tier caching (local + distributed)
Deploy request coalescing middleware

title: Log Rotation Failure
symptoms: Application performance degrading from large log writes
root_causes: logrotate configuration syntax error
Application holding file descriptor of rotated log
Insufficient disk space for compressed archive
Permission issues on log directory
resolution_steps: 1. Test logrotate config: `logrotate -d /etc/logrotate.d/<service>`
2. Force rotation: `logrotate -f /etc/logrotate.d/<service>`
3. Signal application to reopen log files: `kill -USR1 <PID>`
4. Fix permissions: `chown syslog:adm /var/log/<service>/`
5. Manually compress and truncate oversized logs
6. Verify cron job for logrotate is active
prevention: Use stdout/stderr logging with container log drivers
Test logrotate config in deployment pipeline
Monitor log file sizes with alerts
Implement application-level log rotation (e.g., RotatingFileHandler)

title: Authentication Service Failure
root_causes: Auth service crashed or out of memory
LDAP / Active Directory server unreachable
JWT signing key rotation failure
Database backend for auth service is down
resolution_steps: 1. Check auth service health: `curl http://auth-svc:8080/health`
2. Verify LDAP connectivity: `ldapsearch -H ldap://<host> -x`
3. Restart auth service: `systemctl restart auth-service`
4. Verify JWT signing keys are valid and accessible
5. Check auth database connectivity
6. Temporarily enable bypass for critical services if needed
prevention: Deploy auth service with high availability (3+ replicas)
Implement token caching to survive short outages
Monitor auth service health and latency
Maintain emergency access procedures

title: Load Balancer 502 Bad Gateway
root_causes: All backend servers down or unhealthy
Backend servers overloaded and not responding in time
Health check misconfiguration
Network connectivity issue between LB and backends
resolution_steps: 1. Check upstream health: `curl -I http://<backend>:<port>/health`
2. Review LB config: verify upstream server list and ports
3. Check backend service logs for errors
4. Increase upstream timeout if backends are slow
5. Restart unhealthy backend instances
6. Scale up backend pool if under capacity
prevention: Configure proper health check endpoints
Set appropriate timeout and retry values
Implement circuit breakers between LB and backends
Use auto-scaling for backend services

title: Disk I/O Bottleneck
symptoms: High I/O wait (%iowait) in CPU stats
Database queries slow despite low CPU
root_causes: Too many concurrent disk operations
HDD instead of SSD for high-IOPS workloads
Noisy neighbour on shared storage
Filesystem fragmentation
resolution_steps: 1. Check I/O stats: `iostat -xz 1 5`
2. Identify I/O-heavy processes: `iotop -o`
3. Move hot data to faster storage tier (SSD/NVMe)
4. Tune I/O scheduler: `echo deadline > /sys/block/sda/queue/scheduler`
5. Offload logging to separate volume
6. Implement write batching and async I/O in application
prevention: Use SSD/NVMe for database and high-IOPS workloads
Monitor disk I/O metrics and set alerts
Separate data and log volumes
Provision IOPS according to workload requirements

title: NFS Mount Stale
symptoms: df command hangs for NFS mounts
root_causes: NFS server rebooted without client remount
Network interruption between client and NFS server
NFS export configuration changed
Server-side filesystem error
resolution_steps: 1. Check NFS server status: `showmount -e <nfs-server>`
2. Try lazy unmount: `umount -l /mnt/nfs`
3. Remount: `mount -a` or `mount <nfs-server>:/export /mnt/nfs`
4. If hung: force unmount `umount -f /mnt/nfs`
5. Restart NFS client services: `systemctl restart nfs-client.target`
6. Verify NFS exports on server: `exportfs -v`
prevention: Use `soft,timeo=30,retrans=3` mount options
Monitor NFS mount health with synthetic checks
Implement autofs for automatic remount on failure
Consider object storage for large-scale shared data

title: Kubernetes Pod CrashLoopBackOff
symptoms: Container repeatedly exiting with non-zero code
root_causes: Application crash on startup (misconfiguration)
Missing required environment variables or secrets
Liveness probe misconfigured (too aggressive)
OOM kill due to insufficient memory limits
resolution_steps: 1. Check pod events: `kubectl describe pod <pod>`
2. View container logs: `kubectl logs <pod> --previous`
3. Check exit code: 137 = OOM, 1 = app error, 127 = binary not found
4. Verify env vars and secrets: `kubectl get secret <name> -o yaml`
5. Adjust liveness probe: increase initialDelaySeconds and timeoutSeconds
6. Fix root cause and redeploy
prevention: Set appropriate resource requests and limits
Configure startup probes for slow-starting containers
Validate manifests with kube-linter before deployment
Test container startup locally with same env vars

title: Redis Connection Refused
symptoms: Application fallback to database causing overload
root_causes: Redis process crashed or was killed
Max connections limit reached
Network policy blocking port 6379
Redis running out of memory (OOM protection enabled)
resolution_steps: 1. Check Redis status: `redis-cli ping` or `systemctl status redis`
2. Review Redis logs: `tail -100 /var/log/redis/redis-server.log`
3. Check connections: `redis-cli INFO clients | grep connected_clients`
4. Restart Redis: `systemctl restart redis`
5. Verify memory: `redis-cli INFO memory`
6. Check network policies / firewall: `ss -tlnp | grep 6379`
prevention: Deploy Redis Sentinel or Cluster for HA
Set `maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru`
Monitor Redis memory and connection count
Implement connection pooling in application clients

title: GPU Out-of-Memory
symptoms: hipMalloc or cudaMalloc returns out-of-memory error
GPU process crash with 'OOM' in ROCm dmesg
Pod status: OOMKilled on GPU node
Inference latency spikes followed by failures
root_causes: Batch size too large for available VRAM
Memory leak in GPU kernel (unreleased tensors)
Too many models loaded simultaneously on same GPU
Fragmented VRAM after repeated allocations and deallocations
resolution_steps: 1. Check VRAM usage: `rocm-smi --showmeminfo vram` or `nvidia-smi`
2. Identify offending process: `rocm-smi --showpid`
3. Reduce batch size or model parallelism
4. Restart the GPU workload pod
5. Enable memory defragmentation: reboot GPU node if necessary
6. Set VRAM resource requests and limits in pod spec
prevention: Specify VRAM limits in container resource spec
Add GPU memory monitoring dashboards
Implement model batching with adaptive sizing
Run memory profiling on inference workloads
Use MIG or GPU partitioning for multi-tenant isolation

title: ROCm Driver Failure
symptoms: rocminfo fails to find any GPU devices
dmesg shows 'AMDGPU: Fatal error during GPU init'
GPU compute pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
Kernel module amdgpu fails to load
root_causes: ROCm driver version incompatible with kernel
GPU ECC uncorrectable error causing driver panic
Firmware mismatch between GPU VBIOS and driver
Insufficient hugepage allocation for GPU DMA
resolution_steps: 1. Check driver status: `dmesg | grep -i amdgpu`
2. Verify ROCm installation: `rocminfo`
3. Run GPU diagnostics: `amd-smi -a`
4. Reinstall or downgrade ROCm driver
5. Check kernel compatibility: `uname -r` vs supported kernel list
6. Reboot node and re-probe amdgpu module
prevention: Pin ROCm driver version to validated kernel versions
Test driver upgrades in staging before production
Monitor GPU health with ROCm telemetry
Maintain fallback driver version for quick rollback
Document kernel compatibility matrix for all GPU nodes

title: PostgreSQL Replication Lag
symptoms: Read replicas serving stale data minutes behind primary
WAL generation rate exceeds replica replay rate
root_causes: Long-running write transaction on primary generating excessive WAL
Replica under-provisioned (CPU/IO slower than primary)
Network latency between primary and replicas
Vacuum or ANALYZE on primary causing WAL spike
resolution_steps: 1. Check lag: `SELECT now() - pg_last_xact_replay_timestamp() AS replication_lag;`
2. Identify WAL sources: `SELECT * FROM pg_stat_statements ORDER BY total_time DESC LIMIT 10;`
3. Cancel long-running write transactions on primary
4. Temporarily increase replica resources (CPU/memory)
5. Tune max_wal_size, wal_keep_size, and checkpoint_completion_target
6. Add more replicas or use cascading replication for read scaling
prevention: Set up replication lag monitoring and alerts (threshold: 60s)
Provision replicas with at least equal resources as primary
Use synchronous replication for critical writes
Schedule VACUUM and ANALYZE during off-peak hours
Implement read-after-write consistency in application via primary reads

title: Credential Rotation Failure
symptoms: Services returning 401/403 after scheduled key rotation time
Automation job for key rotation reports failure
Vault agent unable to authenticate to secrets backend
root_causes: Rotation automation script failed silently (permissions, network, or token expiry)
Vault token used for rotation had expired before rotation window
Dependency on external service (HSM, KMS) during rotation
Credential still cached in running services
resolution_steps: 1. Identify the failed credential from alerts: `vault list secret/credentials`
2. Manually rotate the credential via vault/secrets manager UI
3. Verify new credential works: `vault read secret/credentials/<name>`
4. Restart affected services or trigger reload
5. Check rotation automation logs for failure root cause
6. Re-run rotation validation test suite
prevention: Store rotation tokens with auto-renewal in Vault
Implement staged rotation with canary validation
Set up credential-age monitoring and alerts
Use short-lived credentials with automatic refresh (STS, OIDC)
Test rotation automation in staging environment weekly

title: SSRF Attack Response
symptoms: Alerts from metadata service access attempts (169.254.169.254)
Suspicious outbound connections to internal IP ranges
Logs showing URL parameters containing internal IPs or file:// schemes
Security scanner or WAF detecting SSRF probe patterns
root_causes: Application accepts user-supplied URLs and fetches them server-side
Missing allowlist for permitted external domains
No input validation on URL scheme and host
Metadata service (IMDS) v1 enabled allowing token-less access
resolution_steps: 1. Block all outbound traffic to 169.254.169.254 at the firewall/WAF
2. Rotate any potentially exposed cloud provider credentials
3. Disable IMDSv1; enforce IMDSv2 with hop limit
4. Patch application: add URL allowlist, block private IP ranges
5. Deploy endpoint to validate internal IPs before outbound fetches
6. Run vulnerability scan to confirm SSRF vectors are mitigated
prevention: Enforce IMDSv2 with Hop Limit=1 on all instances
Maintain strict URL allowlist for server-side fetches
Block private IP ranges in application-level URL validation
Run DAST scanning for SSRF in CI/CD pipeline
Segment metadata service access via network policies

title: Ransomware Incident Response
symptoms: Files on shared storage renamed with .locked or .encrypted extension
Ransom note text files appearing in directories
Storage volume IOPS spike followed by access denied errors
Backup jobs failing with checksum mismatch
root_causes: Compromised user credentials with write access to shares
Unpatched server exposed to internet with SMB/NFS vulnerability
Backup storage mounted read-write at time of encryption
Phishing attack delivered ransomware to employee workstation
resolution_steps: 1. Isolate affected systems from network immediately
2. Identify patient zero from authentication logs
3. Preserve encrypted files and ransom note for forensics
4. Restore data from immutable/air-gapped backups
5. Verify backup integrity before restoring services
6. Reset all credentials for affected accounts
7. Engage incident response and law enforcement if required
prevention: Maintain immutable and air-gapped backups
Implement least-privilege access to shared storage
Deploy endpoint detection and response (EDR) on all servers
Disable SMBv1 and restrict NFS exports by IP
Run tabletop exercises for ransomware scenarios quarterly

title: BGP Route Hijack
symptoms: Traffic to production IP ranges drops or routes to unexpected destinations
External monitoring reports timeouts from multiple geographic locations
BGP looking-glass shows more-specific routes from unknown ASNs
Users report inability to reach the service
root_causes: Transit provider or peering partner leaked routes
Misconfigured route advertisement from internal router
Malicious BGP hijack from attacker ASN
Lack of RPKI filtering on upstream routers
resolution_steps: 1. Identify hijacked prefixes from BGP looking-glass services
2. Contact upstream providers to filter malicious routes
3. Announce more-specific prefix to override hijack
4. Deploy RPKI ROA (Route Origin Authorization) to prevent future hijacks
5. Implement BGP prefix filtering with IRR data
6. Monitor BGP updates with a BGP monitoring service
prevention: Register RPKI ROAs for all owned IP prefixes
Filter inbound BGP advertisements from peers
Monitor BGP announcements with a BGP monitoring tool
Use prefix-list filters on all eBGP sessions
Participate in MANRS (Mutually Agreed Norms for Routing Security)

title: Garbage Collection Tuning
symptoms: Application latency spikes every few minutes
GC logs show frequent Full GC or pause times > 5 seconds
CPU utilization spikes corresponding to GC events
Application throughput drops during GC pauses
root_causes: Heap size too small for workload object allocation rate
Incorrect GC algorithm selection for application profile
Object allocation rate exceeds GC throughput
Memory leak causing tenured generation to fill up
resolution_steps: 1. Enable GC logging: `-Xlog:gc*:gc.log:time,uptime,tags`
2. Analyze GC log with GCeasy or GCViewer
3. Identify pause frequency, duration, and heap occupancy
4. Tune heap size: set -Xms and -Xmx to equal value
5. Switch GC algorithm: G1GC for low-pause, ZGC for sub-millisecond
6. Profile object allocation rate and optimize hot allocation paths
prevention: Set GC metrics monitoring (pause time, frequency, heap usage)
Run load tests with GC logging enabled before releases
Use G1GC with -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200 for latency-sensitive apps
Implement heap dump on OOM for post-mortem analysis
Review object allocation patterns in code review

title: Prometheus Database Corruption
symptoms: Prometheus crashes with 'TSDB corruption detected' error
Missing metrics data for recent time window
Queries return incomplete or incorrect results
root_causes: Unclean shutdown of Prometheus (kill -9, node crash)
Disk errors causing corrupted WAL or TSDB blocks
Out-of-disk space during TSDB compaction
Bug in Prometheus TSDB WAL implementation
resolution_steps: 1. Check Prometheus logs for corruption details
2. Run TSDB repair: `promtool tsdb repair /path/to/data`
3. If repair fails, delete corrupted blocks: `rm -rf /data/*/corrupted-block-id`
4. Restart Prometheus and verify WAL replay
5. Rebuild data from remote storage (Thanos/Cortex) if available
6. Set up Thanos sidecar for HA metric storage
prevention: Deploy Prometheus with graceful shutdown handling
Use persistent volumes with monitoring for disk health
Set up Thanos or Cortex for long-term HA metric storage
Enable WAL compression to reduce corruption surface
Backup Prometheus TSDB data regularly

title: Message Broker Partition Recovery
symptoms: Producers receive timeout or leader-not-available errors
Messages published but not consumed for extended period
root_causes: Broker disk failure causing replica loss
Network partition isolating broker from cluster
Unclean leader election losing committed messages
Memory pressure causing broker to drop connections
resolution_steps: 1. Check cluster health: `kafka-broker-api-versions.sh --bootstrap-server <host>`
2. Identify affected partitions: `kafka-topics.sh --describe --under-replicated-partitions`
3. Restart failed broker if it rejoins cluster
4. Reassign replicas: `kafka-reassign-partitions.sh`
5. Reset consumer offsets if needed: `kafka-consumer-groups.sh --reset-offsets`
6. Verify data integrity and resume producers
prevention: Set min.insync.replicas=2 and replication.factor=3
Prevent unclean leader election: `unclean.leader.election.enable=false`
Monitor partition health and ISR (in-sync replicas) count
Provision brokers with adequate disk and memory resources
Test partition recovery procedure in chaos engineering drills

title: Container Image Vulnerability Response
symptoms: Image vulnerability scanner reports CVSS 9+ vulnerabilities
Kubernetes admission webhook blocking deployments due to policy
Trivy/Grype scan output shows known-exploitable CVEs
Security advisory from Docker/Package registry about base image
root_causes: Base image (Alpine, Ubuntu, Distroless) has known CVEs
Application dependency with known vulnerability not updated
Image built from outdated base layer in CI pipeline
No image scanning in deployment pipeline
resolution_steps: 1. Identify vulnerable images: `trivy image <repo>/<name>:<tag>`
2. Pin exact base image version with known-good SHA
3. Rebuild image with patched base and dependencies
4. Scan rebuilt image to confirm vulnerabilities resolved
5. Deploy patched image and roll out gradually
6. Verify no running containers still using vulnerable image
prevention: Scan all images in CI/CD pipeline (fail on critical CVEs)
Use minimal base images (distroless, scratch)
Regularly rebuild images to pick up security patches
Run container runtime security (Falco, AppArmor, seccomp)
Subscribe to CVE notifications for all dependencies

title: Backup Validation and Recovery
symptoms: Scheduled backup job completes with warnings or errors
Restore test fails with checksum mismatch
Backup size anomaly (significantly smaller or larger than usual)
Alert from backup monitoring: 'backup integrity check failed'
root_causes: Disk corruption on backup storage target
Intermittent network failure during backup transfer
Inconsistent snapshot due to missing filesystem freeze
Backup software bug or configuration error
resolution_steps: 1. Check backup job logs for error details
2. Verify source data integrity with checksum tool
3. Test restore from secondary backup copy
4. If secondary is corrupt, identify last known-good backup
5. Re-run backup after fixing underlying issue
6. Verify restored data with application-level validation
prevention: Automate backup integrity verification (restore test) weekly
Use 3-2-1 backup rule: 3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite
Monitor backup success/failure with alerting
Immutable backups to protect against ransomware
Document and test recovery procedures regularly
